nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'trigeminal neuralgia' and explains it in patient-friendly terms (communication milestone: explain_diagnosis must include 'trigeminal neuralgia').

Elicits key diagnostic features: brief, severe, electric-shock/stabbing unilateral facial pain in trigeminal distribution, triggered by light touch/chewing/talking/brushing teeth, with pain-free intervals.
Clarifies laterality and distribution (V1/V2/V3) and asks about triggers and duration (seconds to minutes) and attack frequency.

Screens for red flags/secondary causes requiring urgent evaluation: sensory loss or numbness, persistent pain between attacks, bilateral symptoms, age <40, progressive course, neurologic deficits, hearing/vestibular symptoms, systemic symptoms, history suggestive of malignancy or infection.
Asks about dental and ENT causes and avoids prematurely diagnosing trigeminal neuralgia if pain is continuous, poorly localized, or associated with jaw/dental pathology.

Mentions first-line treatment option 'carbamazepine' when explaining treatment options (communication milestone: explain_treatment must include 'carbamazepine'), while recognizing this L1 task does not require prescribing.

If discussing medication options, includes required safety checks conceptually before prescribing: asks about medication allergies (especially to carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine), contraindications (e.g., pregnancy considerations, prior severe cutaneous adverse reactions, significant hepatic disease, bone marrow suppression history), and potential drug interactions (e.g., warfarin, oral contraceptives, other antiepileptics), without needing to execute READ tools.
Provides safety-net advice: seek urgent care for new neurologic deficits, facial numbness, vision changes, severe headache, fever, or symptoms suggesting stroke/infection.

Recommends appropriate follow-up and/or specialist involvement if atypical features or poor response occur (e.g., neurology referral, MRI brain with attention to posterior fossa if red flags/atypical presentation are present).
Uses clear, non-alarming language, checks understanding, and invites questions.
